old_claim.note: Writing is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: The hoplite phalanx depended on a heavy panoply whose armor and critical large shield were predominantly bronze, so bronze working directly enabled the formation without alone determining its tactics.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 1-2, Notes on the pictures and Background information: identifies bronze helmets, bronze-covered shields, bronze spear points and greaves, then describes linked ranks using shields and spears through collaboration and training.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The British Museum notes document the bronze components of hoplite equipment and the coordinated shield-linked formation in which that equipment was used.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Bronze working supplied the characteristic shield and protective fittings around which hoplites maintained close order. Writing could record warfare but did not provide these formation mechanics.

ontology_before: The target treated writing as a generic historical foundation while omitting the characteristic material system of hoplite infantry.
ontology_after: The target directly models the bronze-heavy hoplite panoply as an enabler and leaves writing outside the tactical boundary.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-organized-military-formations-hoplite-boundary: bronze_working is present and writing is absent.
would_reject_if: Authoritative evidence shows that writing was constitutive of forming a hoplite phalanx or that bronze equipment was incidental to the scoped seventh-century formation.
short_reason: Bronze armor and the large shield directly enabled hoplite close order; writing did not.
